event: The hominin 'Lucy' (Australopithecus afarensis) lives in the Afar region, her fossil later discovered at Hadar in 1974.
Early anatomically modern humans (Homo sapiens idaltu) inhabit the Middle Awash region, among the oldest known of our species.
Post-glacial communities in the Ethiopian Highlands begin domesticating local crops such as teff and ensete.
Ethiopian tradition holds that Menelik I, legendary son of King Solomon and the Queen of Sheba, founds the Solomonic line.
The Kingdom of D'mt flourishes in northern Ethiopia and Eritrea, leaving monumental temples and inscriptions with Sabaean influence.
The Kingdom of Aksum rises as a major trading power linking the Roman world, Arabia, and India via the Red Sea port of Adulis.
Aksum begins minting its own gold, silver, and bronze coinage, a sign of its wealth and statehood.
King Ezana converts to Christianity, making Aksum one of the world's first Christian states.
Aksumite king Kaleb invades Himyar in Yemen, briefly extending Ethiopian power across the Red Sea.
The Aksumite king grants asylum to early Muslim refugees from Mecca in the first Hijra, a landmark in Islamic-Ethiopian relations.
Tradition holds that Queen Gudit (Yodit) sacks Aksum and topples the ancient dynasty, marking the kingdom's decline.
The Zagwe dynasty consolidates power, shifting Ethiopia's center southward to Lasta.
King Lalibela commissions the famous rock-hewn churches at Roha, a new Jerusalem and a marvel of medieval architecture.
Yekuno Amlak overthrows the Zagwe and restores the Solomonic dynasty, which claims descent from King Solomon.
Emperor Amda Seyon I expands the empire and wages successful campaigns against neighboring Muslim sultanates.
Imam Ahmad ibn Ibrahim al-Ghazi (Ahmad Gran) of Adal invades the Christian highlands, devastating the empire.
Emperor Gelawdewos, aided by Portuguese musketeers, defeats and kills Ahmad Gran at the Battle of Wayna Daga.
Emperor Fasilides founds Gondar as a permanent capital, ushering in a flourishing era of castle-building and the arts.
The death of Emperor Iyasu II accelerates imperial decline, leading toward the fragmented Zemene Mesafint (Era of the Princes).
Tewodros II is crowned emperor, reuniting the country and ending the Era of the Princes.
A British expedition storms Magdala; Emperor Tewodros II takes his own life rather than surrender.
Yohannes IV is crowned emperor and defends Ethiopia against Egyptian and Mahdist incursions.
Menelik II becomes emperor and signs the Treaty of Wuchale with Italy, whose disputed terms spark conflict.
Ethiopia decisively defeats Italy at the Battle of Adwa, securing its independence and inspiring anti-colonial movements worldwide.
Ras Tafari Makonnen becomes regent and heir, beginning his rise to power.
Ethiopia joins the League of Nations, gaining international recognition.
Ras Tafari is crowned Emperor Haile Selassie I, taking the throne name that inspires the Rastafari movement.
Haile Selassie promulgates Ethiopia's first written constitution.
Fascist Italy under Mussolini invades Ethiopia in the Second Italo-Ethiopian War.
Italy occupies Addis Ababa; Haile Selassie goes into exile and appeals to the League of Nations.
British and Ethiopian forces liberate the country, and Haile Selassie returns to his throne.
Eritrea is federated with Ethiopia under a UN resolution, an arrangement Ethiopia later dismantles.
Addis Ababa hosts the founding of the Organisation of African Unity, with Ethiopia as a key continental leader.
A military junta, the Derg, deposes Haile Selassie, ending the Solomonic monarchy.
Mengistu Haile Mariam seizes control of the Derg, launching the brutal Red Terror against opponents.
With Soviet and Cuban support, Ethiopia repels Somalia's invasion in the Ogaden War.
A catastrophic famine kills hundreds of thousands and prompts the global Live Aid relief effort.
The EPRDF rebel coalition overthrows Mengistu, who flees into exile, ending the Derg regime.
Eritrea formally secedes and gains independence after a UN-supervised referendum.
A new constitution establishes the Federal Democratic Republic of Ethiopia with ethnic-based federalism, and Meles Zenawi becomes prime minister.
A border dispute ignites the deadly Eritrean-Ethiopian War, which kills tens of thousands.
Ethiopia and Eritrea sign the Algiers Agreement ending their border war.
Ethiopia becomes a founding member of the African Union, successor to the OAU, headquartered in Addis Ababa.
Construction begins on the Grand Ethiopian Renaissance Dam on the Blue Nile, a flagship national development project.
Prime Minister Meles Zenawi dies in office and is succeeded by Hailemariam Desalegn.
Abiy Ahmed becomes prime minister, launches sweeping reforms, and makes peace with Eritrea.
Abiy Ahmed is awarded the Nobel Peace Prize for ending the conflict with Eritrea.
Civil war erupts in the Tigray region between federal forces and the TPLF, causing a major humanitarian crisis.
A peace agreement signed in Pretoria halts the devastating two-year Tigray War.
